original_abstract:
Today, the technology for video streaming over the Internet is converging
towards a paradigm named HTTP-based adaptive streaming (HAS). HAS comes with
two unique flavors. First, by riding on top of HTTP/TCP, it leverages the
network-friendly TCP to achieve firewall/NATS traversal and bandwidth sharing.
Second, by pre-encoding and storing the video in a number of discrete bitrate
levels, it introduces video bitrate adaptivity in a scalable way that the video
encoding is excluded from the closed-loop adaptation. A conventional wisdom is
that the TCP throughput observed by a HAS client indicates the available
network bandwidth, thus can be used as a reliable reference for the video
bitrate selection.
 We argue that this no longer holds true when HAS becomes a substantial
fraction of the Internet traffic. We show that when multiple HAS clients
compete at a network bottleneck, the presence of competing clients and the
discrete nature of the video bitrates would together create confusion for a
client to correctly perceive its fair-share bandwidth. Through analysis and
real experiments, we demonstrate that this fundamental limitation would lead
to, for example, video rate oscillation that negatively impacts the video
watching experiences. We therefore argue that it is necessary to implement at
the application layer a "probe-and-adapt" mechanism for HAS video rate
adaptation, which is akin but orthogonal to the transport-layer network rate
adaptation achieved by TCP. We present PANDA -- a client-side rate adaptation
algorithm for HAS -- as an embodiment of this idea. Our testbed results show
that compared to conventional algorithms, PANDA is able to reduce the
instability of video rate by 60%, at a given risk of buffer underrun.
humanized_abstract: Currently, most video streaming over Internet is implemented with a new technology named HTTP-based adaptive streaming (HAS). This technology has two aspects: 1) riding on top of HTTP/TCP for firewall/NATS traversal and bandwidth sharing; and 2) using pre-encoded videos with multiple discrete bitrates for scalable video bitrate adaptivity while keeping the resource-demanding video encoding out of the feedback loop. It is a common belief that the client's observed TCP throughput can be used as the signal for the available bandwidth which is then used to guide the decision for the video bitrate. In this paper, we investigate this belief and show that it can be invalid for current Internet when HAS becomes one of the dominant traffics on the Internet. In case of a cross point where multiple HAS clients share the same outgoing link, and given the fact that every client is empowered to choose any of the bitrates available in the stream (which are usually discretely distributed) from its server, the other sharing clients and the discrete choice of bitrates at each client's side confuses the client about what could be its fair-share bandwidth for the shared link. Experiments verify the drawbacks of the current HAS client implementation, and we further demonstrate the necessity of an improved mechanism in this case. We propose an "probe-and-adapt" solution at the application layer similar to, yet complementary to, the transport-layer network rate adaptation provided by TCP. We implement aHAS client side algorithm PANDA (Probe-And-Adapt-for-Network-Dispersions-and-Async) to demonstrate the merits of such application-layer adaptation for current HAS implementation. The extensive experiments carried out with our testbed confirm the reduced video rate instability achieved with PANDA as well as the acceptability of the increased risk of buffer underrun (such as stalling for 1-5 seconds) at the cost of this improvement. The improved client-side rate adaptation forHAS reduces video rate oscillation by up to 60% at the expense of up to 5% more chance of a 1-5 seconds-long buffer underrun event.
